event: The Last Day of Pompeii — destruction by volcanic eruption
symbols: volcanic fire raining down — divine or cosmic judgment upon a city
collapsing buildings — the impermanence of human civilization
fleeing, terrified people — the futility of escape from overwhelming destruction
fallen bodies — the finality of death amid catastrophe
darkness and fire — the imagery of apocalyptic judgment
description: A dramatic, large-scale painting depicting the destruction of Pompeii during the eruption of Mount Vesuvius. The scene is chaotic and terrifying — buildings collapse on the left amid clouds of ash and volcanic debris. The sky is dark and red with fire and smoke. Groups of terrified figures flee in all directions, some carrying children or possessions. Bodies of the dead and dying litter the street in the foreground. Lightning or volcanic fire streaks across the sky. The lighting is lurid, with red and orange tones from the volcanic glow contrasting with the deep darkness of the ash clouds.
theological_description: While the destruction of Pompeii in 79 AD is a historical event rather than a biblical narrative, the scene powerfully evokes the biblical theology of divine judgment upon wicked cities. The volcanic destruction bears unmistakable parallels to the destruction of Sodom and Gomorrah, when 'the LORD rained down burning sulfur on Sodom and Gomorrah — from the LORD out of the heavens' (Genesis 19:24). Jesus Himself used Sodom's destruction as a warning about the coming day of judgment: 'It will be more bearable for Sodom on the day of judgment than for you' (Matthew 11:24). The apocalyptic imagery of fire, darkness, and collapsing structures mirrors the eschatological visions of Revelation: 'The cities of the nations collapsed... Every island fled away and the mountains could not be found' (Revelation 16:19-20). Peter used the destruction of Sodom as a paradigm of God's judgment to come: 'if he condemned the cities of Sodom and Gomorrah by burning them to ashes, and made them an example of what is going to happen to the ungodly' (2 Peter 2:6). The terrified flight of the Pompeians echoes the prophetic warnings: 'Flee from the wrath to come' (Matthew 3:7).

scripture_refs.reason: The destruction of Sodom by fire and brimstone — the direct biblical parallel to Pompeii's volcanic destruction
Sodom's destruction as an example of judgment on the ungodly — Pompeii as a historical echo
Cities collapsing and mountains fleeing — the eschatological destruction this scene evokes
Fleeing from the wrath to come — the terrified citizens escaping the volcanic destruction
